facts.text: A standard symphony orchestra typically has between 70 and 100 musicians divided into four sections: strings, woodwinds, brass, and percussion.
The violin is the smallest and highest-pitched member of the string family and is typically made from spruce and maple wood.
Universal Music Group is the largest music company in the world, owning labels like Interscope, Def Jam, and Republic Records.
The Grammy Awards were first held on May 4, 1959, at the Beverly Hilton Hotel in Los Angeles.
A musical scale is a set of notes ordered by pitch, with the most common Western scales being major and minor.
The Stradivarius violins, crafted by Antonio Stradivari in the 17th and 18th centuries, are considered the finest ever made and can sell for millions of dollars.
Motown Records was founded by Berry Gordy Jr. in Detroit in 1959 and became one of the most influential record labels in music history.
The French horn is one of the most difficult brass instruments to play, with a tubing length of about 12 to 13 feet when uncoiled.
Auto-Tune was invented by Andy Hildebrand, a former engineer for Exxon who used signal processing algorithms originally designed for interpreting seismic data.
The Suzuki method of music education, developed by Shinichi Suzuki in Japan, teaches children to play instruments by ear before learning to read music.
The Billboard Hot 100 chart has been the standard music popularity chart in the United States since it was established in 1958.
The cello, short for violoncello, is tuned in fifths to the notes C-G-D-A from low to high.
Quincy Jones has been nominated for 80 Grammy Awards and won 28, making him one of the most decorated musicians in Grammy history.
The oboe is a double-reed woodwind instrument that is often used to tune the orchestra because its pitch is very stable and penetrating.
Woodstock, the iconic music festival, took place on August 15-18, 1969, on a dairy farm in Bethel, New York, attracting an estimated 400,000 people.
The term 'tempo' refers to the speed of a piece of music, with common markings including Largo (very slow), Andante (walking pace), Allegro (fast), and Presto (very fast).
The harp is one of the oldest instruments in the world, with evidence of harp-like instruments dating back to 3500 BCE in ancient Mesopotamia.
Spotify, launched in 2008 in Sweden, reached over 600 million users worldwide and fundamentally changed how people consume music.
The timpani, also called kettledrums, are the most common percussion instruments in a symphony orchestra and can be tuned to specific pitches.
The Lennon-McCartney songwriting partnership produced over 180 jointly credited songs for The Beatles between 1962 and 1970.
The bassoon is a large double-reed woodwind instrument with a tube length of about 8 feet, folded in half to make it manageable to play.
Rick Rubin co-founded Def Jam Recordings in his NYU dorm room in 1984 and went on to produce iconic albums across genres from hip-hop to country.
A chord is a group of three or more notes played simultaneously, with the most basic types being major and minor triads.
The Rock and Roll Hall of Fame, located in Cleveland, Ohio, inducts new members annually and opened its doors on September 2, 1995.
The flute is one of the oldest known musical instruments, with bone flutes dating back approximately 43,000 years found in German caves.
Atlantic Records was founded in 1947 by Ahmet Ertegun and Herb Abramson and became a powerhouse for jazz, R&B, soul, and rock.
The double bass is the largest and lowest-pitched bowed string instrument in the orchestra, standing about 6 feet tall.
Phil Spector developed the 'Wall of Sound' production technique in the 1960s, using large groups of musicians playing together to create a dense, layered effect.
Music therapy is a clinical and evidence-based practice that uses music interventions to address physical, emotional, cognitive, and social needs of individuals.
The clarinet has the largest pitch range of any common woodwind instrument, spanning nearly four octaves.
Live Aid, held on July 13, 1985, was a dual-venue benefit concert in London and Philadelphia that raised over $125 million for Ethiopian famine relief.
The trombone is unique among brass instruments because it uses a slide rather than valves to change pitch.
The term 'vinyl' refers to polyvinyl chloride (PVC), the material used to make phonograph records since the late 1940s.
Juilliard School in New York City, founded in 1905, is one of the world's most prestigious performing arts conservatories.
A time signature tells musicians how many beats are in each measure and what note value gets one beat, with 4/4 being the most common.
The trumpet has been used for signaling and ceremony for over 3,500 years, with ancient examples found in Egypt's tomb of Tutankhamun.
The Moog synthesizer, introduced in the 1960s by Robert Moog, was the first widely used electronic synthesizer and revolutionized music production.
Burt Bacharach and Hal David wrote dozens of hit songs together, including 'Raindrops Keep Fallin' on My Head' and 'What the World Needs Now Is Love.'
The viola is slightly larger than the violin and tuned a fifth lower, providing the alto voice of the string section in an orchestra.
An octave is the interval between one musical pitch and another with double or half its frequency, spanning eight notes in a diatonic scale.
Columbia Records, founded in 1887, is the oldest surviving brand name in recorded sound and has been home to artists from Miles Davis to Beyoncé.
The tuba is the largest and lowest-pitched brass instrument in the orchestra, with some models weighing over 35 pounds.
Max Martin, a Swedish songwriter and producer, has written or co-written more number-one hits on the Billboard Hot 100 than any other songwriter except Paul McCartney.
The piano has 88 keys — 52 white and 36 black — and a range of over seven octaves, making it one of the most versatile instruments.
The phonograph was invented by Thomas Edison in 1877, making it possible to record and play back sound for the first time.
Musical notation as we know it today was largely developed by Guido of Arezzo, an Italian monk, around 1000 CE.
The sitar, a plucked string instrument from India, became widely known in Western music after Ravi Shankar's collaborations with George Harrison in the 1960s.
A key signature in music indicates which notes are to be played sharp or flat throughout a piece, simplifying notation.
Berklee College of Music in Boston is the largest independent college of contemporary music in the world.
The 8-track tape cartridge was popular in the United States from the mid-1960s through the early 1980s, commonly used in car stereos.
Dr. Dre's album 'The Chronic' (1992) helped define West Coast hip-hop and established him as one of the most influential producers in music.
The djembe is a rope-tuned skin-covered drum from West Africa that has become one of the most popular world percussion instruments.
Medieval music (500-1400 CE) was primarily vocal and monophonic, with Gregorian chant being one of the most well-known forms.
The compact disc (CD) was commercially released on October 1, 1982, in Japan, with Billy Joel's '52nd Street' being the first album released on CD.
The conductor of an orchestra uses a baton to set the tempo, indicate dynamics, and cue different sections of the ensemble.
Sony Music Entertainment is the second largest music company in the world, with labels including Columbia, RCA, and Epic Records.
Brian Eno, known as the father of ambient music, also produced landmark albums for U2, Talking Heads, and David Bowie.
The pentatonic scale, containing five notes per octave, is found in musical traditions across the globe from China to Scotland to West Africa.
The first MTV Video Music Awards (VMAs) were held on September 14, 1984, at Radio City Music Hall in New York.
The English horn is neither English nor a horn — it's actually an alto oboe, a double-reed woodwind instrument pitched a fifth below the oboe.
Warner Music Group, the third-largest music company, owns labels including Warner Bros. Records, Elektra, and Parlophone.
The concept of harmony — multiple notes sounding simultaneously — developed in Western music primarily during the Medieval period around the 9th century.
Pro Tools, first released in 1991, became the industry standard digital audio workstation (DAW) for professional recording studios.
The piccolo is the highest-pitched instrument in the orchestra, sounding one octave higher than the standard flute.
Rod Temperton, who wrote Michael Jackson's 'Thriller,' 'Rock with You,' and 'Off the Wall,' was a former member of the British funk band Heatwave.
The Celtic harp, also known as the clàrsach, has been a symbol of Ireland for centuries and appears on Irish coins and the Guinness logo.
Renaissance music (1400-1600) saw the rise of polyphony, where multiple independent melodic lines are performed simultaneously.
The first Grammy Award for Record of the Year was given to Domenico Modugno for 'Nel Blu Dipinto Di Blu (Volare)' in 1959.
The Roland TR-808 drum machine, released in 1980, became one of the most influential instruments in music history despite being a commercial failure upon release.
A crescendo is a gradual increase in loudness, while a decrescendo (or diminuendo) is a gradual decrease.
The Royal Academy of Music in London, founded in 1822, is the oldest conservatoire in the United Kingdom.
Cassette tapes were introduced by Philips in 1963 and dominated music distribution through the 1970s and 1980s.
The contrabassoon, or double bassoon, plays an octave lower than the standard bassoon and is the lowest-pitched woodwind in the orchestra.
Quincy Jones produced Michael Jackson's 'Thriller,' which remains one of the best-selling albums of all time with over 70 million copies sold.
African drumming traditions, including polyrhythmic patterns and call-and-response structures, heavily influenced jazz, blues, rock, and virtually all American popular music.
The term 'fortissimo' (ff) means to play very loudly, while 'pianissimo' (pp) means to play very softly.
Island Records, founded by Chris Blackwell in Jamaica in 1959, was instrumental in bringing reggae music to international audiences.
The cornet is similar to the trumpet but has a more conical bore, giving it a warmer, mellower tone often preferred in brass bands.
Ableton Live, first released in 2001, revolutionized electronic music production by allowing real-time composition and performance.
Yo-Yo Ma, born in Paris in 1955, is widely considered the greatest living cellist and has won 19 Grammy Awards.
The blues scale adds a 'blue note' (flatted fifth) to the minor pentatonic scale, creating the distinctive sound of blues music.
The MP3 format was developed by the Fraunhofer Society in Germany and became publicly available in 1993, revolutionizing digital music distribution.
Baroque music (1600-1750) is characterized by ornate musical ornamentation, with major composers including Bach, Handel, and Vivaldi.
A&M Records, founded by Herb Alpert and Jerry Moss in 1962, operated from Charlie Chaplin's former Hollywood studio for decades.
The glockenspiel is a percussion instrument consisting of tuned metal bars played with hard mallets, producing a bright, bell-like tone.
George Martin, known as the 'Fifth Beatle,' produced nearly all of The Beatles' recordings and was crucial to their innovative studio sound.
Raga, the melodic framework used in Indian classical music, can contain specific rules about which notes to emphasize and what time of day the raga should be performed.
The treble clef, also known as the G clef, is the most commonly used clef in Western music notation.
Apple Music launched on June 30, 2015, as Apple's streaming music service to compete with Spotify and other platforms.
The xylophone's name comes from the Greek words 'xylon' (wood) and 'phone' (voice), as its bars are made of wood unlike the metal glockenspiel.
Sub Pop Records, founded in Seattle in 1986, helped launch the grunge movement by signing Nirvana, Soundgarden, and Mudhoney.
The harmonic series is a sequence of frequencies that are integer multiples of a fundamental frequency and determines the timbre of musical instruments.
Nile Rodgers, guitarist and producer, has produced hits for Diana Ross, David Bowie, Madonna, and Daft Punk spanning four decades.
The marimba is a large wooden percussion instrument similar to the xylophone but with resonator tubes beneath each bar for a richer, deeper sound.
Classical period music (1730-1820) emphasized clarity, balance, and form, with Mozart, Haydn, and early Beethoven as its key figures.
The first Woodstock festival featured 32 acts over four days and became a defining moment of the 1960s counterculture movement.
The cor anglais produces a more melancholic and darker tone than the oboe and is featured prominently in Dvořák's 'New World Symphony.'
The Grammy Award for Album of the Year has been given every year since 1959, with Stevie Wonder being the first artist to win three times.
Solfège — the system of do, re, mi, fa, sol, la, ti — originated from a Latin hymn for Saint John the Baptist in the 11th century.
The legendary concert at Monterey Pop Festival on June 16-18, 1967, helped launch the careers of Jimi Hendrix, Janis Joplin, and The Who in America.
The snare drum gets its distinctive buzzing sound from metal wires (snares) stretched across the bottom drumhead.
Rough Trade Records, an independent label founded in London in 1978, was pivotal in the UK indie music scene and signed The Smiths.
The whole tone scale contains only whole steps with no half steps, creating an ethereal, dreamlike quality used by composers like Debussy.
Timbaland pioneered a production style that blended hip-hop beats with influences from Indian, Middle Eastern, and electronic music.
The triangle, despite being one of the simplest orchestral instruments, is notoriously difficult to play well and its part requires precise timing.
The Romantic period in music (1820-1900) emphasized emotional expression and individualism, with composers like Chopin, Liszt, Wagner, and Tchaikovsky.
The first gold record was awarded to Perry Como's 'Catch a Falling Star' on March 14, 1958, by the RIAA.
The celesta is a keyboard instrument that strikes metal plates with hammers, producing a delicate, bell-like sound famously heard in Tchaikovsky's 'Dance of the Sugar Plum Fairy.'
Lee 'Scratch' Perry, a Jamaican producer, pioneered dub music by using the recording studio as an instrument with echo, reverb, and delay effects.
The gamelan is a traditional ensemble from Indonesia consisting primarily of tuned percussion instruments including metallophones, xylophones, and gongs.
A fermata is a symbol in music notation that tells the performer to hold a note or rest longer than its usual duration, at the performer's discretion.
Def Jam Recordings, co-founded by Rick Rubin and Russell Simmons in 1984, helped bring hip-hop into the mainstream with artists like Run-DMC, LL Cool J, and Beastie Boys.
The French horn evolved from hunting horns and was introduced into the orchestra during the Baroque period in the late 17th century.
Napster, launched on June 1, 1999, was the first major peer-to-peer file sharing service and disrupted the music industry's distribution model.
Itzhak Perlman, one of the greatest violinists of the 20th century, has performed at multiple presidential inaugurations and won 16 Grammy Awards.
The kora is a 21-string West African instrument that bridges the harp and lute families and is traditionally played by griots (storytellers).
Syncopation is a rhythmic technique where emphasis is placed on normally weak beats or off-beats, creating a sense of surprise or groove.
The 45 RPM single was introduced by RCA Victor in 1949 and became the standard format for hit singles for decades.
The tubular bells are long metal tubes hung in a frame and struck with a mallet to create a church bell-like sound in the orchestra.
Daniel Lanois, a Canadian producer known for his atmospheric sound, produced landmark albums for U2, Bob Dylan, and Peter Gabriel.
Carnatic music, one of the two main traditions of Indian classical music, originated in South India and emphasizes vocal performance.
The bass clef, also known as the F clef, is used for lower-pitched instruments like the cello, bassoon, trombone, and tuba.
The Concert for Bangladesh, organized by George Harrison on August 1, 1971, was the first large-scale benefit concert in music history.
The cymbals used in orchestras are most commonly made of a bronze alloy (roughly 80% copper, 20% tin).
Stax Records in Memphis was the home of Southern soul music in the 1960s and 1970s, releasing music by Otis Redding, Isaac Hayes, and Booker T. & the M.G.'s.
Perfect pitch, the ability to identify or produce any musical note without a reference tone, is estimated to occur in only 1 in 10,000 people.
The oud is a short-necked, fretless stringed instrument central to Middle Eastern and North African music traditions.
Counterpoint is the art of combining two or more independent melodic lines, perfected by J.S. Bach in works like 'The Art of Fugue.'
The Glastonbury Festival, first held in 1970 in Somerset, England, has become the world's largest greenfield music and performing arts festival.
The harpsichord was the dominant keyboard instrument before the piano and differs in that its strings are plucked rather than struck.
Berry Gordy founded Motown Records with an $800 loan from his family and built it into the most successful Black-owned business in America.
Flamenco music from Spain combines singing (cante), guitar playing (toque), dance (baile), handclaps (palmas), and finger snapping (pitos).
The circle of fifths is a visual representation of the relationships among the twelve tones of the chromatic scale.
iTunes Store launched on April 28, 2003, and sold one million songs in its first week, proving digital music sales were viable.
The vibraphone, or vibes, is a metal-barred percussion instrument with motor-driven rotating discs in the resonator tubes that create a vibrato effect.
Chess Records in Chicago was crucial to the development of blues, rhythm and blues, and rock and roll, recording Muddy Waters, Chuck Berry, and Howlin' Wolf.
The didgeridoo, an Australian Aboriginal wind instrument, is one of the oldest instruments still in use, with some estimates placing its origin at 1,500 years ago or more.
Clive Davis, longtime head of Columbia Records and later Arista and J Records, is credited with discovering or nurturing Whitney Houston, Alicia Keys, and Barry Manilow.
A cadence is a sequence of chords that brings a musical phrase to a point of rest — the most final-sounding being the 'authentic cadence' (V-I).
The Royal Conservatory of Music in Toronto has graded music examinations that are used by over 500,000 students annually across North America.
The bass drum is the largest drum in an orchestra and produces the lowest, most resonant sound in the percussion section.
The first recording to sell one million copies was Enrico Caruso's 1902 recording of 'Vesti la giubba' from the opera Pagliacci.
Maqam is the system of melodic modes used in traditional Arabic, Turkish, and Persian music, with each maqam evoking a specific emotion.
Dynamics in music range from ppp (pianississimo, extremely soft) to fff (fortississimo, extremely loud), with mp and mf in the middle range.
U2's 360° Tour (2009-2011) grossed over $736 million and is one of the highest-grossing concert tours in history.
The cor anglais earned its misleading name possibly through a corruption of 'cor anglé' (angled horn), referring to the bend in early versions of the instrument.
Phil Ramone, known as the 'Pope of Pop,' produced recordings for artists ranging from Frank Sinatra to Billy Joel to Paul Simon and won 14 Grammy Awards.
The shakuhachi is a Japanese end-blown bamboo flute used in traditional music and Zen Buddhist meditation practice.
An arpeggio is a chord whose notes are played in succession rather than simultaneously, creating a rippling or cascading effect.
The first commercial music CD ever sold was Billy Joel's '52nd Street,' released in Japan on October 1, 1982.
The tam-tam is a large, flat gong used in orchestras that produces a deep, resonant, and ominous sound — it is different from a tuned gong.
Muscle Shoals Sound Studio in Alabama produced hit recordings for Aretha Franklin, The Rolling Stones, and Bob Seger despite being in a tiny rural town.
Bossa nova, which emerged in Brazil in the late 1950s, blends samba rhythms with jazz harmonies and was popularized internationally by João Gilberto and Antonio Carlos Jobim.
The Italian terms for standard tempo markings — Adagio, Andante, Moderato, Allegro, Vivace, Presto — have been used in music since the 17th century.
Elvis Presley's concert 'Aloha from Hawaii' on January 14, 1973, was the first entertainment special to be broadcast live via satellite worldwide.
The English horn section of an orchestra sits with the oboe players, as it is essentially a larger, lower-pitched version of the oboe.
Sun Records, founded by Sam Phillips in Memphis in 1952, launched the careers of Elvis Presley, Johnny Cash, Jerry Lee Lewis, and Carl Perkins.
Absolute (or perfect) pitch appears to be more common among speakers of tonal languages like Mandarin Chinese and Vietnamese.
Linda Perry, known for writing 'What's Up?' with 4 Non Blondes, became one of pop's most sought-after producers, working with P!nk, Christina Aguilera, and Gwen Stefani.
Transposition is the process of moving a collection of notes up or down in pitch by a fixed interval.
The Grammy Awards ceremony is organized by the Recording Academy and recognizes achievements in the music industry across dozens of categories.
The viola da gamba, a fretted string instrument held between the legs, was popular during the Renaissance and Baroque periods before being replaced by the cello.
Thomas Edison recorded the first audio in history — 'Mary Had a Little Lamb' — on a tinfoil cylinder phonograph on November 21, 1877.
The balalaika is a triangular-bodied Russian stringed instrument that comes in various sizes from piccolo to contrabass.
Pharrell Williams produced or co-wrote hits for artists across multiple genres, including Justin Timberlake, Snoop Dogg, Britney Spears, and Daft Punk.
A fugue is a compositional technique where a theme is introduced by one voice and then successively taken up by others, a form perfected by J.S. Bach.
Coachella Valley Music and Arts Festival, held annually in Indio, California since 1999, has become one of the world's most famous music festivals.
The clarinet was invented around 1700 by Johann Christoph Denner in Nuremberg, Germany, based on an earlier instrument called the chalumeau.
The Billboard 200 album chart was first published on March 24, 1956, and has tracked album sales, streaming, and downloads ever since.
Fela Kuti, a Nigerian musician and political activist, created Afrobeat by fusing West African music with jazz, funk, and highlife.
Ted Templeman produced classic albums for Van Halen, The Doobie Brothers, and Van Morrison, helping define the California rock sound.
The chromatic scale contains all twelve pitches within an octave, each separated by a half step or semitone.
The three tenors — Luciano Pavarotti, Plácido Domingo, and José Carreras — first performed together at the 1990 FIFA World Cup concert in Rome.
The saxophone, invented by Adolphe Sax around 1840, is a woodwind instrument despite being made of brass because it uses a single-reed mouthpiece.
The RIAA Gold certification for a single means 500,000 units sold, while Platinum means one million units.
The erhu, a two-stringed Chinese fiddle, is sometimes called the 'Chinese violin' and can produce an astonishingly wide range of expressive tones.
Stock Aitken Waterman (SAW) was a British songwriting-production trio that dominated the pop charts in the late 1980s, producing over 100 UK Top 40 hits.
A whole note lasts four beats in 4/4 time, a half note lasts two beats, a quarter note one beat, and an eighth note half a beat.
The Royal Albert Hall in London, opened in 1871, hosts over 400 events each year including the famous BBC Proms concert series.
The concert pitch standard of A=440 Hz was internationally adopted in 1955, though earlier standards varied from 415 Hz to 450 Hz.
Arif Mardin, a Turkish-American producer, worked with artists from Aretha Franklin and The Bee Gees to Norah Jones, winning 12 Grammys across five decades.
Tango music from Argentina combines European musical elements with African-influenced rhythms brought by enslaved people to the Río de la Plata region.
The term 'a cappella' means 'in the style of the chapel' in Italian and refers to vocal music performed without instrumental accompaniment.
The first iPod was announced by Apple on October 23, 2001, with the slogan '1,000 songs in your pocket,' transforming portable music.
The tam-tam differs from the gong in that it has no definite pitch, while tuned gongs produce a clear note when struck.
Interscope Records, founded in 1990, became one of the most successful labels of the 1990s and 2000s with Dr. Dre, Eminem, and Lady Gaga.
The glass harmonica, invented by Benjamin Franklin in 1761, uses rotating glass bowls and was once banned in some European towns because people believed its sound caused madness.
Jimmy Jam and Terry Lewis, former members of The Time, became one of the most successful production duos in R&B history, producing 16 number-one hits for Janet Jackson alone.
Modulation is the process of changing from one key to another within a piece of music, often used to create emotional shifts.
Carnegie Hall in New York City opened on May 5, 1891, with a concert conducted by Tchaikovsky and remains one of the world's most prestigious concert venues.
The euphonium is a brass instrument similar to but smaller than a tuba, with a warm, rich tone often featured in concert bands.
Blue Note Records, founded in 1939, became the premier jazz label, recording iconic albums by Miles Davis, John Coltrane, Thelonious Monk, and Art Blakey.
The theremin, invented in 1920 by Russian physicist Leon Theremin, is played without physical contact — the musician moves their hands near two metal antennas.
Mutt Lange, a South African-born producer, created the massive sound of albums by AC/DC ('Back in Black'), Def Leppard ('Hysteria'), and Shania Twain ('Come On Over').
The circle of fifths shows that starting on any note and going up by perfect fifths eventually returns you to the starting note after twelve steps.
The Berlin Philharmonic, founded in 1882, is considered one of the finest orchestras in the world.
The oboe is traditionally made from grenadilla wood (African blackwood) and requires players to craft their own reeds.
The Yamaha Corporation started as a piano and reed organ manufacturer in 1887 and grew into one of the world's largest musical instrument companies.
The mbira, also known as the thumb piano, is a traditional instrument from Zimbabwe consisting of metal tines attached to a wooden board.
Staccato is a musical articulation that makes notes short and detached, indicated by a dot above or below the note head.
Live Nation Entertainment and Ticketmaster merged in 2010, creating a company that dominates live music promotion and ticketing worldwide.
The piccolo trumpet, pitched an octave above the standard trumpet, is often used for Baroque music including Bach's 'Brandenburg Concerto No. 2.'
The first Brit Awards ceremony was held on October 18, 1977, celebrating 100 years of recorded sound in Britain.
The banjo was brought to America by enslaved Africans and became central to Appalachian folk music, bluegrass, and early jazz.
David Foster, a Canadian producer and songwriter, has won 16 Grammy Awards and produced for Whitney Houston, Celine Dion, Michael Bublé, and Josh Groban.
An interval is the distance between two pitches, measured in steps — a minor third (3 half-steps), major third (4 half-steps), perfect fifth (7 half-steps).
The Vienna Philharmonic, based at the Musikverein in Vienna, is famous for its annual New Year's Concert broadcast to over 90 countries.
The bass clarinet is a larger version of the clarinet pitched one octave lower, with a distinctive upturned bell, and is used in both orchestras and jazz.
The first music video played on MTV was 'Video Killed the Radio Star' by The Buggles at 12:01 AM on August 1, 1981.
Legato means to play notes smoothly and connectedly, the opposite of staccato, often indicated by a curved line (slur) over the notes.
T Bone Burnett has produced and supervised the music for numerous films including 'O Brother, Where Art Thou?,' 'Walk the Line,' and 'Crazy Heart.'
The Nashville Number System is a method of transcribing music by denoting chords with numbers rather than letters, making it easy to transpose songs.
Summerfest in Milwaukee, Wisconsin, is recognized by Guinness World Records as the world's largest music festival, running for 11 days each summer.
The contrabass clarinet is the largest member of the clarinet family, standing about 8 feet tall and sounding two octaves below the standard clarinet.
Verve Records, founded by Norman Granz in 1956, became a premier jazz label with artists including Ella Fitzgerald, Stan Getz, and Oscar Peterson.
The Ondes Martenot, an early electronic instrument invented in 1928 by Maurice Martenot, was used extensively by composer Olivier Messiaen.
Tony Visconti, an American-born producer based in London, is renowned for his work with David Bowie and T. Rex across multiple decades.
The church modes — Dorian, Phrygian, Lydian, Mixolydian, Aeolian, Locrian, and Ionian — predate the modern major and minor scale system.
Bob Geldof organized Live Aid after seeing a BBC news report on the Ethiopian famine, ultimately inspiring the largest televised music event of its era.
The flugelhorn resembles a trumpet but has a wider, conical bore that produces a darker, more mellow sound favored in jazz.
The Recording Industry Association of America (RIAA) has certified music sales in the US since 1958.
The steel drum (or steel pan) was invented in Trinidad and Tobago in the 1930s and is the only acoustic musical instrument invented in the 20th century.
Danger Mouse (Brian Burton) produced albums for The Black Keys, Gorillaz, and the controversial 'Grey Album' mashing up Jay-Z and The Beatles.
Figured bass was a Baroque-era notation system where numbers beneath a bass line indicated which chords the keyboard player should improvise.
Red Rocks Amphitheatre near Denver, Colorado, is a naturally formed outdoor concert venue with 9,525 seats, renowned for its acoustics and stunning geology.
The Wagner tuba was designed for Richard Wagner's 'Ring Cycle' operas and combines characteristics of the French horn and tuba.
The first commercially successful vinyl record player (the Victrola) was introduced by the Victor Talking Machine Company in 1906.
The samba, originating in Brazil's Afro-Brazilian communities, features syncopated rhythms played on instruments like the surdo, tamborim, and pandeiro.
In music, 'rubato' means to expressively and freely stretch or compress the tempo of a passage without changing the overall pace.
The Sydney Opera House, opened on October 20, 1973, is one of the most iconic concert venues in the world and a UNESCO World Heritage Site.
The alto flute sounds a fourth lower than the concert flute and has a characteristically mellow, haunting tone.
Rough Trade Records signed The Strokes and Arctic Monkeys, helping launch the indie rock revival of the early 2000s.
Guzheng, a Chinese zither with 21 strings, has been played for over 2,500 years and remains one of China's most popular traditional instruments.
Nellee Hooper, a British producer, produced Björk's debut album 'Debut' and Massive Attack's 'Blue Lines,' helping define the trip-hop genre.
An ostinato is a short musical pattern that is repeated persistently throughout a composition, commonly found in both classical and popular music.
The Hollywood Bowl in Los Angeles, built in 1922, is one of the largest natural amphitheaters in the world with a seating capacity of nearly 18,000.
The modern piano was invented by Bartolomeo Cristofori in Italy around 1700, originally called the 'pianoforte' because it could play both soft and loud.
Sony's Walkman, introduced on July 1, 1979, was the first portable personal stereo cassette player and transformed how people listened to music.
A tritone is an interval of three whole tones (six half steps) that was called 'diabolus in musica' (the devil in music) during the Medieval period.
Sylvia Massy, known for her unconventional techniques, has produced albums for Tool, System of a Down, and Red Hot Chili Peppers.
AABA form (32-bar form) was the standard structure for popular songs from the 1920s through the 1950s, used in countless jazz standards.
The Montreux Jazz Festival, held annually in Switzerland since 1967, has become one of the most prestigious music festivals in the world.
The bass trombone has a larger bore and bell than the tenor trombone and includes one or two valves to extend its lower range.
Decca Records famously rejected The Beatles in 1962, telling their manager Brian Epstein that guitar groups were 'on the way out.'
Ranchera is a genre of traditional Mexican music that often features mariachi accompaniment and themes of love, patriotism, and nature.
The diminished seventh chord, containing four notes each a minor third apart, creates maximum tension and was a favorite device of Romantic-era composers.
Bruce Swedien, Michael Jackson's recording engineer, spent nearly a year mixing 'Thriller' and developed innovative techniques to capture Jackson's voice.
The concert grand piano is the largest type of piano, typically about 9 feet long, and produces the fullest, most resonant tone.
Vinyl records have experienced a major resurgence, with US vinyl sales surpassing CD sales for the first time since 1987 in the early 2020s.
The shamisen is a three-stringed Japanese instrument played with a large plectrum called a bachi and is central to traditional Japanese theater.
Nigel Godrich, often called the sixth member of Radiohead, has produced every Radiohead album since 'OK Computer' and also worked with Paul McCartney.
A drone is a sustained note or chord that continues throughout a piece of music, commonly found in bagpipe music, Indian classical music, and medieval chant.
The Fillmore in San Francisco, operated by promoter Bill Graham, was the epicenter of 1960s psychedelic rock and hosted nearly every major act of the era.
The E-flat clarinet is smaller and higher-pitched than the standard B-flat clarinet and produces a sharper, more piercing tone in orchestral music.
The first digital download to sell one million copies was Gwen Stefani's 'Hollaback Girl' in 2005.
Qawwali is a form of Sufi devotional music from the Indian subcontinent, with Nusrat Fateh Ali Khan being its most famous practitioner worldwide.
Alberti bass is a common accompaniment pattern in keyboard music where the notes of a chord are played in the order: lowest, highest, middle, highest.
Ed Sullivan's television show introduced many musicians to American audiences, most famously The Beatles on February 9, 1964, watched by 73 million viewers.
The Steinway concert grand piano, Model D, has been the instrument of choice for the vast majority of concert pianists since the 19th century.
XL Recordings, a British independent label, has been home to Adele, Radiohead, The Prodigy, and The White Stripes.
The bouzouki, a long-necked stringed instrument from Greece, is essential to rebetiko music and was later adopted into Irish folk music.
Butch Vig produced Nirvana's 'Nevermind' and co-founded the band Garbage, becoming one of the key architects of the alternative rock sound.
The twelve-tone technique (or serialism), developed by Arnold Schoenberg in the 1920s, organizes all twelve chromatic pitches into a fixed sequence.
The three biggest music companies — Universal, Sony, and Warner — collectively control approximately 70% of the global recorded music market.
Cor anglais reeds are wider and shorter than oboe reeds and are attached to a curved metal tube called a bocal.
The first Grammy Awards had only 28 categories; today there are over 90 categories recognizing diverse genres and achievements.
Highlife is a genre of music that originated in Ghana in the early 20th century, blending traditional Akan music with Western instruments and jazz.
A musical canon is a contrapuntal technique where an initial melody is imitated by one or more voices at a fixed interval of time, like 'Row, Row, Row Your Boat.'
Bob Ludwig is one of the most successful mastering engineers in history, having mastered albums for Led Zeppelin, Nirvana, Bruce Springsteen, and Daft Punk.
The celesta was patented by Auguste Mustel in Paris in 1886 and Tchaikovsky was one of the first major composers to use it.
Wax cylinder recordings, introduced in the 1880s, were the first commercial format for recorded music before being replaced by flat disc records.
The accordion, invented in Austria in the early 19th century, became a staple in folk music traditions from France to Argentina to Mexico.
Jack Antonoff has become one of the most in-demand pop producers of the 2020s, producing albums for Taylor Swift, Lana Del Rey, and Lorde.
The dominant seventh chord (V7) is the most common type of seventh chord in Western music and naturally resolves to the tonic chord.
The Kennedy Center Honors, presented annually since 1978, recognize lifetime contributions to American culture through the performing arts.
The rotary valve trumpet is preferred by many European orchestras for its warmer tone, while American orchestras generally use piston valve trumpets.
Emile Berliner patented the first flat disc gramophone record in 1887, replacing Edison's cylindrical format and creating the template for vinyl records.
The pan flute, made from graduated tubes bound together, has been used in music traditions from the Andes to Romania to ancient Greece.
Trevor Horn, known for producing Frankie Goes to Hollywood's 'Relax' and Yes's '90125,' pioneered the use of sampling technology in pop production.
The Neapolitan sixth chord is a major chord built on the flatted second degree of the scale, commonly used in Classical and Romantic music for dramatic effect.
The Grand Ole Opry, broadcasting from Nashville since 1925, is the longest-running radio broadcast in US history and the spiritual home of country music.
The alto saxophone in E-flat is the most commonly played saxophone in both classical and popular music settings.
The Latin Grammy Awards were first held on September 13, 2000, recognizing excellence in Latin music across various genres and regions.
The hurdy-gurdy is a stringed instrument that produces sound by a hand-cranked wheel rubbing against the strings, functioning like a mechanical violin.
Mark Ronson produced Amy Winehouse's 'Back to Black' and Bruno Mars's 'Uptown Funk,' both of which became defining songs of their respective decades.
A glissando is a continuous slide from one pitch to another, achievable on instruments like the trombone, harp, and piano.
The Apollo Theater in Harlem, New York, has been central to African-American music since 1934 and launched careers through its famous Amateur Night.
Violin strings were historically made from catgut (actually sheep or goat intestine), though modern strings typically use steel, synthetic, or wound metal cores.
The Fender Stratocaster, introduced in 1954, is one of the most iconic and widely copied electric guitar designs in music history.
Griot musicians in West Africa serve as oral historians, storytellers, and praise singers, maintaining their communities' histories through music for centuries.
Greg Kurstin, who co-wrote and produced Adele's 'Hello,' won Producer of the Year at the Grammys multiple times.
A leitmotif is a recurring musical theme associated with a particular character, place, or idea, a technique perfected by Richard Wagner and later adopted by film composers.
The Sydney Festival of Music was founded to celebrate the opening of the Sydney Opera House in 1973.
The bass flute sounds an octave lower than the concert flute and has a distinctively breathy, atmospheric quality.
The Rock and Roll Hall of Fame requires that an artist's first recording be at least 25 years old before they can be nominated for induction.
The yangqin, a Chinese hammered dulcimer, was likely introduced to China from Persia via the Silk Road around the late Ming Dynasty.
Teddy Riley invented the New Jack Swing genre in the late 1980s, fusing R&B vocals with hip-hop beats for artists like Bobby Brown, Guy, and Michael Jackson.
Figured bass numbers like '6/4' and '6/3' indicated to Baroque keyboard players which chord inversions to play above the written bass note.
The BBC Proms (Promenade Concerts) have been held every summer since 1895, culminating in the famous Last Night of the Proms at the Royal Albert Hall.
The natural horn, without valves, requires players to change pitch entirely through lip technique and hand placement in the bell.
The first album to be certified Diamond (10 million copies) by the RIAA was the Eagles' 'Their Greatest Hits (1971-1975).'
Throat singing (khoomei) from Tuva and Mongolia involves producing two or more pitches simultaneously using different parts of the vocal tract.
Finneas O'Connell, Billie Eilish's brother and producer, recorded their debut album 'When We All Fall Asleep, Where Do We Go?' entirely in his small bedroom studio.
The subdominant chord (IV) and dominant chord (V) are the two most important chords after the tonic (I) in Western tonal harmony.
Elton John's 'Farewell Yellow Brick Road' tour (2018-2023) grossed over $900 million, becoming one of the highest-grossing tours ever.
The maracas are a pair of rattles traditionally made from dried gourds filled with seeds and are central to Latin American and Caribbean music.
The very first Grammy Award ceremony had no live performances or television broadcast — it was a dinner event at the Beverly Hilton Hotel.
Gnawa music from Morocco combines sub-Saharan African rhythms, Berber traditions, and Sufi Islam chanting in ritual healing ceremonies.
Equal temperament tuning, which divides the octave into twelve equal semitones, became standard in Western music during the 18th and 19th centuries.
The Tanglewood Music Center in Massachusetts has been the summer home of the Boston Symphony Orchestra since 1937.
Double bass players can either bow the strings (arco) or pluck them (pizzicato), with pizzicato being more common in jazz and popular music.
Napster was shut down by court order on July 11, 2001, after the RIAA's lawsuit, fundamentally changing the debate around digital music rights.
The kalimba, a modern version of the African mbira, was popularized in Western music by Hugh Tracey in the 1950s and 60s.
The legendary Studio 54 in New York City, which operated from 1977 to 1980, was the epicenter of disco culture.
Mixolydian mode sounds like a major scale with a flatted seventh and is commonly used in rock, blues, and folk music.
The Brit Awards, the British equivalent of the Grammys, are organized by the British Phonographic Industry and broadcast annually.
The Wagner tuba is actually played by French horn players and was specifically created to bridge the gap between the horn and trombone sections.
Sony introduced the world's first portable CD player, the D-50 (Discman), in 1984.
The bagpipes are not exclusively Scottish — versions of the instrument exist in traditions across Europe, the Middle East, and North Africa.
Al Schmitt, a legendary recording engineer, won 20 Grammy Awards working with artists from Frank Sinatra to Ray Charles to Diana Krall.
A grace note is an ornamental note played quickly before the main note, adding embellishment to a musical phrase.
Madison Square Garden in New York has been called 'the world's most famous arena' and has hosted legendary concerts by artists from Elvis to Billy Joel.
The contra-alto clarinet, pitched in E-flat, plays one octave below the alto clarinet and is used primarily in concert bands.
The Billboard Music Awards, re-established in 2011, are based entirely on chart performance data including sales, streaming, and airplay.
The djembe drum can produce three basic sounds — bass, tone, and slap — depending on where and how the hand strikes the head.
Berry Gordy's Hitsville U.S.A., the original Motown recording studio at 2648 West Grand Boulevard in Detroit, is now part of the Motown Museum.
Polytonality is the simultaneous use of two or more keys, employed by 20th-century composers like Stravinsky and Milhaud.
The 52nd Annual Grammy Awards in 2010 were the first to expand the number of nominees in the top categories from five to eight.
First-chair (or principal) positions in major orchestras are highly competitive, with auditions often attracting hundreds of applicants from around the world.
The jukebox became a fixture in American restaurants and bars in the 1940s and 1950s, playing 78 RPM and later 45 RPM records.
The sitar typically has 18 to 21 strings, with six or seven played strings and the remainder being sympathetic strings that resonate in response.
Studio musicians known as 'The Wrecking Crew' played on countless hit records in the 1960s and 70s, including Beach Boys, Sonny & Cher, and Simon & Garfunkel sessions.
The Phrygian mode, with its distinctive half-step between the first and second notes, gives music a Spanish or Middle Eastern flavor.
The JUNO Awards, Canada's premiere music awards, have been presented by the Canadian Academy of Recording Arts and Sciences since 1971.
The harpsichord produces sound by plucking strings with quills, which means it cannot vary dynamics — every note sounds at roughly the same volume.
The first song ever streamed on Spotify when it launched was 'Just Dance' by Lady Gaga.
The Chapman Stick, invented by Emmett Chapman in the 1970s, is a fretboard-tapping instrument that allows simultaneous bass and melody playing.
Butch Walker has produced albums for artists ranging from Fall Out Boy to Taylor Swift to Weezer, earning a reputation as a versatile rock producer.
An anacrusis, or pickup, is one or more notes that precede the first full measure of a piece, as heard in 'Happy Birthday.'
Abbey Road Studios in London, made famous by The Beatles, has been a premier recording facility since its opening in 1931.
The A clarinet, slightly longer than the B-flat clarinet, produces a somewhat darker tone and is preferred in orchestral works by Mozart and Brahms.
The London Symphony Orchestra, founded in 1904, was the first orchestra in Britain to be self-governing, with musicians managing the business side.
A metronome is a device that produces a steady pulse at adjustable tempos, helping musicians practice keeping time accurately.
The Grammy Lifetime Achievement Award has been given to legends including Aretha Franklin, The Beatles, Ella Fitzgerald, and Bob Marley.
The pipe organ is the largest of all musical instruments, with the largest organ in the world at Boardwalk Hall in Atlantic City having over 33,000 pipes.
Hindustani classical music, the North Indian tradition, is built on ragas and talas and emphasizes improvisation more than its Carnatic counterpart.
Sound engineers use equalization (EQ) to boost or cut specific frequency ranges, shaping the tonal balance of a recording.
The mandolin, a small stringed instrument with four pairs of strings tuned in unison, is prominent in bluegrass, classical, and Italian folk music.
The Chicago Symphony Orchestra, founded in 1891, is considered one of the 'Big Five' American orchestras along with New York, Boston, Philadelphia, and Cleveland.
A fermata placed over a bar line indicates a brief silence between sections, giving the music a moment of pause.
The sarod is a fretless stringed instrument used in Hindustani classical music, known for its deep, weighty sound and ability to slide between notes.
Record producer Phil Ramone worked on over 300 albums during his career, including Paul Simon's 'Still Crazy After All These Years.'
The concept of 'absolute music' refers to instrumental music that exists purely for its own sake without reference to a narrative, image, or text.
The Grammy Award for Song of the Year goes to the songwriter(s), while Record of the Year honors the performing artist and production team.
The lyre was one of the most important stringed instruments in ancient Greece and Rome, closely associated with the god Apollo.
A soundboard amplifies the vibrations of stringed instruments like the piano, guitar, and violin, projecting their sound outward.
The New York Philharmonic, founded in 1842, is the oldest symphony orchestra in the United States.
Sampling — taking a portion of an existing recording and reusing it in a new composition — became a defining technique of hip-hop and electronic music.
The oboe d'amore, pitched between the oboe and the English horn, was a favorite of Bach and has a gentle, sweet tone.
The Billboard Hot 100 chart methodology now incorporates streaming data, digital downloads, and radio airplay to determine song rankings.
The concert master (or concertmistress) is the lead violinist of an orchestra and serves as the principal liaison between the musicians and conductor.
A musical motif is a short, recurring musical idea — as few as two notes — that serves as a building block for larger compositions.
The kazoo, patented in the United States in 1883, is a membranophone that modifies the player's hummed voice into a buzzing timbre.
MIDI (Musical Instrument Digital Interface) was introduced in 1983 and allows electronic instruments, computers, and other devices to communicate with each other.
The serpent is a bass wind instrument with an S-shaped body that was used in church and military music from the 16th to the 19th century.
Program music tells a story or paints a picture through instrumental music, as in Mussorgsky's 'Pictures at an Exhibition' or Vivaldi's 'The Four Seasons.'
The Academy of Country Music Awards (ACMs) have been presented annually since 1966, typically held in Las Vegas.
The clavichord, a precursor to the piano, was unique among keyboard instruments in allowing vibrato through varying finger pressure on the key.
Multitrack recording, pioneered by Les Paul in the 1940s, allows each instrument or voice to be recorded on a separate track and mixed independently.
The Vienna Philharmonic did not admit women as permanent members until 1997, making it one of the last major orchestras to do so.
The rebab is a bowed stringed instrument found across the Islamic world, from Morocco to Indonesia, with many regional variations.
A bridge in songwriting is a contrasting section that provides relief from the repetitive verse-chorus structure, often introducing new harmonic or melodic ideas.
The Philadelphia Orchestra, founded in 1900, was the first American orchestra to make an electrical recording (1925) and appear in a feature film (Fantasia, 1940).
The suona is a Chinese double-reed instrument with a loud, penetrating sound used in folk music, military bands, and ceremonies.
Grammy voting members of the Recording Academy must be creative professionals who have contributed to at least six commercially released tracks.
Sound mastering is the final step in music production, preparing the mixed audio for distribution by optimizing volume, EQ, and consistency across tracks.
The Aeolian harp is a stringed instrument played by the wind, named after Aeolus, the Greek god of the wind.
The concept of 'call and response,' originating in African musical traditions, became foundational to gospel, blues, jazz, rock, and hip-hop.
The American Bandstand TV show, hosted by Dick Clark from 1956 to 1989, helped introduce rock and roll and R&B to mainstream American audiences.
The santoor is a hammered dulcimer used in Hindustani classical music, Persian music, and various folk traditions across Central Asia.
The lowest note on a standard piano is A0 at 27.5 Hz, while the highest is C8 at 4,186 Hz.
The Crystal Palace concerts organized by August Manns in London from 1855 to 1901 helped popularize orchestral music among the general public.
The charango, a small Andean stringed instrument traditionally made from armadillo shells, is central to the folk music of Bolivia and Peru.
The Roland TB-303 bass synthesizer, originally a commercial failure in 1981, was later adopted by electronic musicians and became essential to the acid house genre.
A twelve-bar blues progression (I-I-I-I-IV-IV-I-I-V-IV-I-V) is one of the most common chord progressions in popular music.
